title: How Computers Actually Work
key_insight: Everything a computer does is a cascade of electrical signals through logic gates — no magic, just physics.
body:
## How a Computer Actually Works

### The Hardware Stack

```
You type a key
    ↓
Electrical signal sent to CPU
    ↓
CPU decodes the signal using the instruction set
    ↓
Data stored temporarily in registers/cache
    ↓
Result written to RAM
    ↓
Display controller reads RAM → pixels update on screen
```

### CPU: The Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle

The CPU runs this loop billions of times per second:

1. **Fetch** — Get the next instruction from memory
2. **Decode** — Figure out what the instruction means
3. **Execute** — Do it (add numbers, move data, compare values)

That's it. Everything a computer does — from running Chrome to training a neural network — is this loop running very, very fast.

### Memory Hierarchy (Speed vs Cost)

| Level | Name | Speed | Size |
|-------|------|-------|------|
| 1 | CPU Registers | ~1 cycle | Bytes |
| 2 | L1/L2 Cache | ~5 cycles | KB–MB |
| 3 | RAM | ~100 cycles | GB |
| 4 | SSD/HDD | ~100,000 cycles | TB |

AI training is slow partly because GPUs must constantly move data between these layers.

### Why GPUs for AI?

A CPU has 8–32 cores — great for sequential tasks.
A GPU has 10,000+ cores — great for doing the same operation on thousands of numbers at once.

Neural network training is matrix multiplication: multiply two grids of numbers. Each multiplication is independent. A GPU can do all of them simultaneously.

A CPU does it like a line of one cashier serving customers one by one.
A GPU does it like opening 10,000 checkout lanes at once.
common_mistakes: Assuming more RAM means a faster CPU — they're separate
Thinking CPU speed (GHz) is the only performance metric — parallelism matters more for AI
Forgetting that data movement (CPU↔RAM↔GPU) is often the bottleneck, not computation itself
mini_exercise: Why do you think it would be bad to train a large neural network on a CPU instead of a GPU? Think about what operation neural networks repeat most.
real_world_usage: When teams choose cloud infrastructure for AI workloads, they reason from first principles: 'Our model is 14GB, inference needs 16GB VRAM minimum → A10G GPU instance. We need 8 replicas for latency → $X/hour.' Von Neumann architecture is why you can't just 'make the AI think faster' — compute, memory, and bandwidth are separate, fundamental limits.
explain_back_prompt: Explain the fetch-decode-execute cycle in your own words. Then explain why this cycle creates a bottleneck for AI inference, and what modern hardware does to work around it.
